items.foco: Red blood cells without nucleus or organelles
items.title: anucleated erythrocytes
items.contentHtml:
Red blood cells without nucleus or organelles

  • Mature erythrocyte: lacks nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes and endoplasmic reticulum; Its metabolism is anaerobic (glycolysis and pentose pathway).
  • Half-life ~120 days; elimination by splenic macrophages (hemocatheresis).
  • Biconcave shape (disc) optimizes surface/volume ratio for gas exchange and capillary deformability.
  • Trap: presence of nucleus in peripheral blood = erythroblast (pathological: hemolytic anemia, myelofibrosis, leukemia).
  • Glossary: Polychromatophilia: blue-gray erythrocytes (reticulocytes) = bone marrow regeneration.

items.title: Hemoglobin and oxygenation
items.contentHtml:
Hemoglobin: structure and O2 transport

  • Tetrameric structure: 2 α chains (141 aa) and 2 β (146 aa); each subunit contains a heme group with Fe²⁺ that binds O₂ reversibly.
  • Sigmoidal dissociation curve: P50 = 26 mmHg (partial pressure at 50% saturation). Right shift (↓affinity): acidosis, ↑2,3-BPG, ↑temperature. Left shift (↑affinity): alkalosis, ↓2,3-BPG, hypothermia.
  • Transport: O₂ bound to Hb (98.5%), dissolved 1.5%. CO₂: 70% as bicarbonate, 23% bound to Hb (carbaminohemoglobin), 7% dissolved.
  • Trap: Methaemoglobin (Fe³⁺) does not transport O₂; cyanosis at >1.5 g/dL; treatment with methylene blue.

items.title: Leukocyte classification
items.contentHtml:
Leukocytes: granulocytes and agranulocytes

  • Granulocytes: neutrophils (40-70%), eosinophils (1-4%), basophils (<1%). They have specific granules and a lobulated nucleus.
  • Agranulocytes: lymphocytes (20-40%), monocytes (2-8%). No specific granules visible in Romanowsky stain.
  • Absolute count: total leukocytes 4,000-11,000/μL. Differential leukocyte formula.
  • Trap: Left deviation: increase in immature neutrophils (bands) in acute bacterial infection.
  • Glossary: ​​Azurophil granules: primary lysosomes in neutrophils (myeloperoxidase).

items.title: Neutrophils in acute defense
items.contentHtml:
Neutrophils: phagocytosis and first line

  • First line of innate defense; half-life 6-8 h in blood, 1-2 days in tissues.
  • Mechanism: chemotaxis (IL-8, C5a, LTB4), opsonization (IgG, C3b), phagocytosis, degranulation (myeloperoxidase, elastase) and extracellular traps (NETs).
  • Threshold: neutropenia <1,500/μL (<500/μL severe risk of infection).
  • Trap: Neutrophilia >7,000/μL: bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, smoking. Cyclic neutropenia: ELANE mutation.
  • Glossary: ​​Pus: dead neutrophils + necrotic tissue + bacteria.

items.foco: Lymphocytes: B and T specific immunity
items.title: B and T lymphocytes
items.contentHtml:
Lymphocytes: B and T specific immunity

  • B lymphocytes: mature in bone marrow; recognize native antigen; They differentiate into plasma cells (antibodies) and memory B cells.
  • T lymphocytes: mature in the thymus; they express TCR; CD4⁺ (helpers, Th1/Th2/Th17) and CD8⁺ (cytotoxic).
  • Normal count: 1,000-4,800/μL. Lymphocytosis >4,800/μL: viral infection (EBV, CMV), chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
  • Trap: NK cells: innate immunity, lysis without MHC; count included in large granular lymphocytes.
  • Glossary: ​​Immunoglobulins: IgG (80%, placental passage), IgA (mucosa), IgM (first response), IgE (allergy), IgD (B receptor).

items.title: Monocytes and macrophages
items.contentHtml:
Monocytes: precursors of macrophages

  • Circulating monocyte (2-8% leukocytes): 12-72 h in blood; migrates to tissues and differentiates into macrophage (resident or activated) or dendritic cell.
  • Functions: phagocytosis, antigen presentation (MHC-II), cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6).
  • Tissue macrophages: Kupffer cells (liver), osteoclasts (bone), microglia (CNS), alveolar macrophages (lung).
  • Trap: Monocytosis >800/μL: tuberculosis, endocarditis, chronic myelomonocytic leukemia.
  • Glossary: Foam cell: loaded macrophage of lipids (atherosclerosis).

items.title: Platelets and hemostasis
items.contentHtml:
Platelets: megakaryocytic fragments hemostasis

  • Platelets: cytoplasmic fragments of the megakaryocyte (bone marrow); size 2-4 μm; half-life 7-10 days.
  • Normal count: 150,000-450,000/μL. Thrombocytopenia <150,000/μL; hemorrhagic risk if <50,000/μL; spontaneous if <10,000/μL.
  • Function: adhesion (GPIb/IX to von Willebrand factor), aggregation (GPIIb/IIIa to fibrinogen), secretion of granules (ADP, TxA2).
  • Trap: Reactive thrombocytosis >450,000/μL: inflammation, iron deficiency, splenectomy. Essential thrombocythemia: JAK2 mutation.

items.foco: Erythropoiesis: red bone marrow and erythropoietin
items.title: Regulated erythropoiesis
items.contentHtml:
Erythropoiesis: red bone marrow and erythropoietin

  • Process from proerythroblast to mature erythrocyte (~7 days). It occurs in red bone marrow (flat bones, femur/humerus epiphysis).
  • Erythropoietin (EPO): glycoprotein hormone produced in the kidney (peritubular interstitial cells) in response to hypoxia (HIF-1α factor).
  • Threshold: Hb <13 g/dL (male) or <12 g/dL (female). = anemia; Serum EPO increases in hypoproliferative anemia (renal failure: ↓EPO).
  • Trap: Reticulocytes (1-2% erythrocytes): reticulocyte production index corrects maturation; >2.5% = adequate spinal response.

items.title: Plasma cells and antibodies
items.contentHtml:
Plasma cells: antibody production

  • Terminal differentiation of the activated B lymphocyte; basophilic cytoplasm (abundant RER), eccentric nucleus with cartwheel pattern.
  • Produces and secretes immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE, IgD) at a rate of ~10,000 molecules/second.
  • They reside in bone marrow (long life, months-years) and lymphoid tissue; They are responsible for humoral immunity.
  • Trap: Multiple myeloma: monoclonal proliferation of plasma cells; monoclonal peak in proteinogram, lytic bone lesions, anemia.
  • Glossary: ​​Mott cell: plasma cell with Russell inclusions (hyaline immunoglobulin bodies).

items.foco: Basophils and eosinophils: allergy and parasites
items.title: Basophils and eosinophils
items.contentHtml:
Basophils and eosinophils: allergy and parasites

  • Basophils (<1%): granules with histamine, heparin, leukotrienes; IgE receptor FcεRI; They participate in type I allergic reactions (anaphylaxis).
  • Eosinophils (1-4%): granules with major basic protein, peroxidase, neurotoxin; active against helminths and allergies.
  • Threshold: Eosinophilia >500/μL: asthma, allergic rhinitis, parasitosis (Strongyloides, Toxocara), hypereosinophilic syndrome (>1,500/μL).
  • Trap: Basophilia >100/μL: chronic myeloid leukemia (associated with Philadelphia chromosome), polycythemia vera.
